meta.course_context: PCS125 - First major topic covering oscillating systems
meta.professor_methodology: Focus on energy analysis and the relationship between position, velocity, and acceleration
meta.description: Oscillating systems including springs and pendulums, with emphasis on kinematics, dynamics, and energy conservation

patterns.name: Period and Frequency Calculations
patterns.recognition_signal: Questions asking for T, f, or ω of oscillators
patterns.logic: Period depends on system parameters (mass and spring constant for springs; length and g for pendulums) but not on amplitude

efficiency_trick.name: Quick Period Ratio
efficiency_trick.trigger: Comparing periods of two similar systems
steps: Period ratio depends only on ratio of m/k or L

pitfalls.name: Amplitude dependence myth
pitfalls.description: Period does NOT depend on amplitude for ideal SHM

pitfalls.name: Small angle approximation
pitfalls.description: Pendulum formula only valid for θ < 15°

worked_example.title: Spring Period Change
worked_example.problem_latex: A 2 kg mass oscillates with T = 3 s. What is T if mass becomes 8 kg?

solution_steps.reasoning: Period proportional to square root of mass

decision_tree.question: Is the system a spring or pendulum?
Need position, velocity, or acceleration vs time?

professor_tips: Always draw the free body diagram to identify the restoring force
Energy methods are often easier than force analysis for finding velocities
At equilibrium: maximum velocity, zero acceleration
At turning points: zero velocity, maximum acceleration

confidence_checklist: I can write x(t), v(t), and a(t) for SHM
I know the difference between frequency f and angular frequency ω
I can calculate period for both springs and pendulums
I understand energy conservation in oscillating systems
I can solve for phase constant from initial conditions
